# Related Work

IRI prediction models fall broadly into three generations. The first generation, exemplified by the AASHO
Road Test serviceability equations, @paterson1987road's World Bank incremental deterioration models (later
folded into HDM-III/HDM-4), and the M-E Pavement Design Guide's smoothness models [@ara2004smoothness; @vonquintus2001flexible],
uses linear or mildly non-linear regression on a small, hand-selected set of distress and site variables.
@vonquintus2001flexible's companion appendix to the one this paper builds on — Appendix OO-1, for flexible
rather than rigid pavements — established the same distress-based regression form for AC pavements that
Appendix PP later established for JPCP/CRCP, and both remain the operational core of AASHTOWare Pavement ME
today. Numerous follow-on studies refit this linear form to different regions and pavement types: @alomari1995effect
and @mactutis2000investigation examined how individual distress types (cracking, rutting, patching) trade off
against each other in explaining IRI on the AASHO and WesTrack test sections respectively; @khattak2014iri and
@perezacebo2020iri fit comparable regressions specifically for HMA-overlaid and two-lane flexible pavements.
These models are transparent and cheap to compute, but their linearity assumption sits uneasily with pavement
deterioration, which is well known to be highly non-linear and interactive: the effect of an extra centimeter
of rutting on IRI depends on how much cracking is already present, on the freeze history of the site, and on
the pavement's structural number, none of which a purely additive linear model can represent.

The second generation replaced the linear form with flexible learners trained directly on LTPP or state DOT
pavement management data. Early work leaned on artificial neural networks: @choi2004pavement's back-propagation
network and @ziari2016prediction's ANN/GMDH comparison both showed sizeable accuracy gains over linear
regression on flexible-pavement IRI, and @kaya2020statistics benchmarked statistical and AI-based remaining-service-life
models side by side on flexible and composite pavements. @kargahostadi2015framework's comparative framework
for empirical pavement performance models was among the first to argue systematically that model *form*, not
just coefficients, should be selected empirically rather than assumed. Tree ensembles arrived next:
@breiman2001random's random forest, applied to LTPP flexible pavements by @gong2018use with a reported testing-set
$R^2$ of 0.974, and the gradient boosting family popularized by tools like @ke2017lightgbm's LightGBM and
applied to asphalt-overlay performance by @zhang2020analysis, consistently outperformed linear M-E benchmarks
in the literature on raw prediction accuracy, at the cost of interpretability: a 400-tree ensemble does not
hand an engineer a coefficient to reason about.

The third generation, to which both @song2022efficient's study and the present work belong, restores
interpretability without sacrificing the ensemble's accuracy advantage, using SHapley Additive exPlanations
[@lundberg2017unified]. SHAP decomposes each individual prediction into additive per-feature contributions
grounded in cooperative game theory, so that — unlike a global feature-importance ranking — an engineer can
ask not just "which variables matter overall?" but "why did the model predict *this* section will roughen
*this* fast?" @song2022efficient used SHAP to show that a simplified six-feature ThunderGBM model retained
most of the accuracy of the full twenty-feature version, a genuinely useful finding for agencies with limited
data-collection budgets.

None of these studies, flexible-pavement or otherwise, (a) apply the same explainable-ensemble approach to
rigid pavements and compare the two families directly, (b) incorporate Falling Weight Deflectometer
joint load-transfer efficiency — the mechanistic quantity that faulting is itself a downstream symptom of —
as a learned feature rather than leaving it implicit, or (c) report accuracy under an evaluation strategy that
accounts for LTPP's repeated-visit panel structure. This study addresses all three gaps.
